3A_Bibliographic.citation: Al-shoaibi, A. A., Helmer, C. K., Shim, J., Choi, W., Dooley, E. E., Gooding, H. C., Gabriel, K. P., Baker, F. C., & Nagata, J. M. (2025). Independent and joint prospective associations of screen time and sleep disturbance with body mass index and waist circumference among U.S. adolescents. Childhood Obesity, 21(7), 618-628.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: Not explicitly stated; data from baseline (2016-2018) and year 2 follow-up
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We analyzed prospective data from 7445 adolescents in the longitudinal Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study... For the current study, we included 7445 participants with complete data for screen time, sleep disturbance, and sleep duration at baseline, and BMI and WC at year 2 of follow-up

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time (total and by modality: TV/movies, video games, socializing)
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time, TV/movies, video games, socializing (texting, video chatting, social media)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (weighted average hours/day of weekday and weekend) and categorical (0-4 low, >4-8 medium, >8 high hours/day)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Participants completed the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey at baseline (2016-2018), reporting the total time spent across all screen-based activities on a typical weekday and weekend day. Screen time was treated as a continuous variable, representing the weighted average in hours. Total recreational screen time was categorized using 4-hour intervals... Screen time was grouped into three categories: 0-4 hours/day (low; reference), >4-8 hours/day (medium), and >8 hours/day (high).

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Body mass index (BMI) and waist circumference (WC) at year 2
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Anthropometric measurement (body weight/height for BMI, tape measurement for WC)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Body weight and height were measured three times, and the average was used. BMI was calculated as body weight (kg) divided by height squared (m2)... WC was measured twice, and the average was computed.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Weighted multivariable linear regression with multiple imputation by chained equations
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race and ethnicity, household income, parent education, study site, physical activity, baseline BMI or WC, pubertal status
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Missing data were imputed using multiple imputation by chained equations... Baseline sex differences were examined, and weighted multivariable linear regression models estimated the independent and joint associations of screen time and sleep disturbance or sleep duration on BMI and WC at year 2... To account for complex survey design and potential nonresponse bias, we incorporated sampling weights derived from the American Community Survey. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Screen time (continuous) was associated with higher BMI (B = 0.32; 95% CI: 0.19, 0.45)
The association with BMI was stronger in males (B = 0.47; 95% CI: 0.29, 0.64)
Screen time was also associated with higher WC in females (B = 0.26; 95% CI: 0.01, 0.52)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Screen time medium (4-8 hrs/day) vs low (0-4)
Screen time high (>8 hrs/day) vs low (0-4)

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: Screen time was significantly associated with higher BMI (B = 0.32; 95% CI: 0.19, 0.45; p < 0.001)
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper uses 'significantly' in abstract. Reports exact CIs throughout. Uses p<0.001 and exact p-values. Does not discuss the magnitude of effect sizes (B=0.32 BMI units per hour of screen time is quite small). No mention of large sample providing power to detect small effects. Discussion focuses on statistical significance and direction of associations.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: These findings support the potential benefit of interventions that reduce screen time and improve sleep hygiene, especially in adolescent males... These findings highlight the need for interventions that reduce screen time and improve sleep quality to offset the risk of obesity.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses on the direction and statistical significance of associations between screen time, sleep, and BMI/WC, particularly highlighting sex differences (stronger in males). It discusses potential biological mechanisms (hormones, appetite regulation) without contextualizing the small magnitude of effects. The conclusion recommends interventions to reduce screen time despite the primary effect being B=0.32 BMI units per hour of daily screen time, which translates to approximately one-third of a BMI point for each additional hour. Effect magnitude is never explicitly discussed.
